concepts.title: Using the method of partial fraction for solving integrals when the denominator has non-repeated roots
concepts.conceptBody:
Using the method of partial fraction for solving integrals when the denominator has non-repeated roots

We have divided this method into 2 types, depending upon the denominator.

  1. If denominator has non repeated factors
  2. If denominator has repeating factors

Type 1: For non-Repeating roots 

When denominator can be expressed as non repeating factors

i.e. $\text{D}\left( \text{x} \right)=\left( \text{x}-{{\alpha }_{1}} \right)\left( \text{x}-{{\alpha }_{2}} \right)\ldots \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ (for linear factors)

$=\left( \text{ax}+\text{bx}+\text{c} \right)\left( \text{p}{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{qx}+\text{r} \right)\ldots \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ quadratic }\!\!~\!\!\text{ factors) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Note that ${{\alpha }_{1}}\ne {{\alpha }_{2}}\ne {{\alpha }_{3}}$

let us understand the concept with illustrations.

Illustration 35 

(for linear factors) 

(a) $\frac{6}{\left( x-1 \right)\left( x+1 \right)}$ (b) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \frac{2\text{x}-3}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}-9}dx$ (c) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \frac{\left( x-1 \right)dx}{\left( 2x+1 \right)\left( x-2 \right)\left( x-3 \right)}$ 

Solution: 

(a) $\int \frac{6}{\left( x-1 \right)\left( x+1 \right)}dx$

for partial fraction method, we write the function as

$\frac{6}{\left( x-1 \right)\left( x+1 \right)}=\frac{A}{\left( x-1 \right)}+\frac{B}{\left( x+1 \right)}$

we have taken A & B as constants as the factors in denominator are linear.

Now we find the values of A & B by solving the R.H.S. & equating the coefficients of L.H.S. & R.H.S.

Numerator in R.H.S. $=\text{A}\left( \text{x}+1 \right)+\text{B}\left( \text{x}-1 \right)$

$=\left( \text{A}+\text{B} \right)\text{x}+\left( \text{A}-\text{B} \right)$

we know there is no term of $\text{x}$ in numerator on L.H.S.

this, equating

$\text{A}+\text{B}=0$ &

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}-\text{B}=6$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $\text{B}=-3,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}=3$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $\frac{6}{\left( \text{x}-1 \right)\left( \text{x}+1 \right)}=\frac{3}{\left( \text{x}-1 \right)}-\frac{3}{\left( \text{x}+1 \right)}$

now solving the integral

$\int ~\frac{6}{\left( x-1 \right)\left( x+1 \right)}dx$ $~=\int ~\frac{3dx}{x-1}-\int ~\frac{3dx}{\left( x+1 \right)}$

$~=3\text{log}\left| x-1 \right|-3\text{log}\left| x+1 \right|+c$

(b) $\int \frac{2\text{x}-3}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}-9}\text{dx}=\int \frac{2\text{x}-3}{\left( \text{x}+3 \right)\left( \text{x}-3 \right)}\text{dx}$

applying partial fractions

$I=\int ~\frac{2x-3}{\left( x+3 \right)\left( x-3 \right)}dx=\int ~\frac{A}{\left( x+3 \right)}+\int ~\frac{B}{x-3}dx$ … (i)

using the method

$\left( 2\text{x}-3 \right)$ $~=\text{A}\left( \text{x}-3 \right)+\text{B}\left( \text{x}+3 \right)$

$~=\left( \text{A}+\text{B} \right)\text{x}+3\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}-\text{A} \right)$

$\Rightarrow $ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}+\text{B}=2$

$\Rightarrow $ A$~=\frac{3}{2},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}=\frac{1}{2}$

putting these values in equation (i)

I $~=\int ~\frac{3/2dx}{x+3}+\int ~\frac{1/2dx}{\left( x-3 \right)}$

$~=\frac{3}{2}\text{log}\left| x+3 \right|+\frac{1}{2}\text{log}\left| x-3 \right|+c$

Trick 

We can also solve the partial fractions by the following easier method.

Note: This method is valid only for linear factors in denominator.

$\frac{2\text{x}-3}{\left( \text{x}-3 \right)\left( \text{x}+3 \right)}=\frac{\text{A}}{\left( \text{x}+3 \right)}+\frac{\text{B}}{\left( \text{x}-3 \right)}$

for A

$\text{A}={{\left. \frac{2\text{x}-3}{\text{x}-3} \right|}_{\text{x}=-3}}=\frac{2\left( -3 \right)-3}{-3-3}=\frac{-9}{-6}=\frac{3}{2}$

this means remove the term under A from denominator & put that root (i.e. $x+\alpha =0$ ) in the term to get the value of $\text{A}$.

Similarly, B

$\text{B}={{\left. \frac{2\text{x}-3}{\text{x}+3} \right|}_{\text{x}=3}}=\frac{2\left( 3 \right)-3}{3+3}=\frac{3}{6}=\frac{1}{2}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{2x-3}{\left( x-3 \right)\left( x+3 \right)}=\frac{3}{2}\left( \frac{1}{x+3} \right)+\frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{1}{x-3} \right)$

which the same result as we got by the previous method.

(c) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \frac{\left( x-1 \right)dx}{\left( 2x+1 \right)\left( x-2 \right)\left( x-3 \right)}$

now by partial fraction

$\frac{\left( x-1 \right)}{\left( 2x+1 \right)\left( x-2 \right)\left( x-3 \right)}=\frac{A}{2x+1}+\frac{B}{\left( x-2 \right)}+\frac{C}{\left( x-3 \right)}$

since there are linear factors, we can use the trick discussed in the previous part.

$A={{\left. \frac{x-1}{\left( x-2 \right)\left( x-3 \right)} \right|}_{x=-1/2}}=\frac{-1/2-1}{\left( -\frac{1}{2}-2 \right)\left( \frac{-1}{2}-3 \right)}=\frac{-6}{35}$

$B={{\left. \frac{x-1}{\left( 2x+1 \right)\left( x-3 \right)} \right|}_{x=2}}=\frac{2-1}{\left( 2\left( 2 \right)+1 \right)\left( 2-3 \right)}=\frac{-1}{5}$

$C={{\left. \frac{x-1}{\left( 2x+1 \right)\left( x-2 \right)} \right|}_{x=3}}=\frac{3-1}{\left( 2\left( 3 \right)+1 \right)\left( 3-2 \right)}=\frac{2}{7}$

Hence the integral becomes

$~=\frac{-6}{35}\int ~\frac{\text{dx}}{\left( 2\text{x}+1 \right)}-\frac{1}{5}\int ~\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{x}-2}+\frac{2}{7}\int ~\frac{\text{dx}}{\left( \text{x}-3 \right)}$

$~=\frac{-6}{35}\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)\text{log}\left| 2\text{x}+1 \right|-\frac{1}{5}\text{log}\left| \text{x}-2 \right|+\frac{2}{7}\text{log}\left| \text{x}-3 \right|+\text{c}$

Illustration 36 

(quadratic factors) 

(a) $\frac{xdx}{\left( x+1 \right)\left( 1+{{x}^{2}} \right)}$ 
(b) $\int \frac{dx}{{{x}^{3}}-1}$ 
(c) $\int \frac{{{x}^{2}}+1}{\left( {{x}^{2}}+2 \right)\left( 2{{x}^{2}}+1 \right)}dx$ 

Solution: 

By quadratic factors we mean that some (not all) factors of the denominator are quadratic, which cannot be factorized further into linear factors and also they are non repeating.

(a) $\int \frac{\text{x}d\text{x}}{\left( \text{x}+1 \right)\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)}$

see here ${{\text{x}}^{2}}+1$ can’t be broken further, for quadratic factor we represent the function as follows.

$\frac{\text{x}}{\left( \text{x}+1 \right)\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)}=\frac{\text{A}}{\left( \text{x}+1 \right)}+\frac{\text{Bx}+\text{C}}{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)}$

Note here that $\text{Bx}+\text{C}$ is linear in nature because the denominator has quadratic factor.

TIP: We heep the degree of numerator one less than that of the denominator. The reason being to be able to use the formula type $\text{{f}'}/\text{f}$

$\therefore $x $~=\text{A}\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)+\left( \text{Bx}+\text{C} \right)\left( \text{x}+1 \right)$

$~=\left( \text{A}+\text{B} \right){{\text{x}}^{2}}+\left( \text{B}+\text{C} \right)\text{x}+\text{A}+\text{C}$

Equating powers on both side we get

A + B = 0 … (i)

B + C = 1 … (ii)

A + C = 0 … (iii)

Solving (i), (ii) & (iii)

$\text{A}=-1/2,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}=\frac{1}{2},\text{C}=\frac{1}{2}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ integral becomes

I $~=\int ~\frac{-1/2dx}{\left( x+1 \right)}+\int ~\frac{\frac{x}{2}+1/2dx}{\left( {{x}^{2}}+1 \right)}$

$~=\frac{-1}{2}\int ~\frac{1}{x+1}dx+\frac{1}{2}\int ~\frac{x+1dx}{{{x}^{2}}+1}$

$~=\frac{-1}{2}\text{log}\left| x+1 \right|+\frac{1}{2}\left[ \int ~\frac{x}{{{x}^{2}}+1}dx+\int ~\frac{1}{{{x}^{2}}+1}dx \right]$

$~=\frac{-1}{2}\text{log}\left| x+1 \right|+\frac{1}{2}\left[ \frac{1}{2}\text{log}\left| {{x}^{2}}+1 \right|+\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x \right]+C$

(b) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\int \frac{dx}{{{x}^{3}}-1}$

here ${{x}^{3}}-1$ can be further factorized, so factorizing it.

${{\text{x}}^{3}}-1=\left( \text{x}-1 \right)\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1 \right)$

now it can’t be further factorized.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\int ~\frac{dx}{\left( x-1 \right)\left( {{x}^{2}}+x+1 \right)}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ using partial fraction

$\frac{1}{\left( x-1 \right)\left( {{x}^{2}}+x+1 \right)}=\frac{A}{\left( x-1 \right)}+\frac{Bx+C}{{{x}^{2}}+x+1}$

$\Rightarrow $1 $~=\text{A}\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1 \right)+\left( \text{Bx}+\text{C} \right)\left( \text{x}-1 \right)$

1$~=\left( \text{A}+\text{B} \right){{\text{x}}^{2}}+\left( \text{A}+\text{C}-\text{B} \right)+\left( \text{A}-\text{C} \right)$

equating powers

A + B = 0 … (i)

A + B = 0 … (ii)

A - C = 1 … (iii)

Solving (i), (ii) & (iii)

$A=\quad 1/3,B=-1/3\quad \text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }\quad C=\frac{-2}{3}$

$\therefore I \quad =\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d x}{x-1}+\int \frac{-1 / 3 x+(-2 / 3) d x}{x^2+x+1}$

$=\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d x}{x-1}-\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{x+2}{x^2+x+1} d x$

$=\frac{1}{3} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{(2 x+1+3)}{x^2+x+1}\right] d x\left\{\text { we will try to break it into } f^{\prime} / f \text { form }\right\}$

$=\frac{1}{3} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 x+1}{x^2+x+1} d x+\int \frac{3 d x}{x^2+x+1}\right]$

$=\frac{1}{3} \log |x-1|-\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{1}{2} \log \left|x^2+x+1\right|\right] \frac{-1}{6} \int \frac{3 d x}{x^2+x+1}$

Leave the last integral, we learn how to solve this type of integral shortly.

(c) $\int \frac{{{x}^{2}}+1}{\left( {{x}^{2}}+2 \right)\left( 2{{x}^{2}}+1 \right)}dx$

here both factors in denominator can not be factorized further.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+1}{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+2 \right)\left( 2{{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)}=\frac{\text{Ax}+\text{B}}{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+2 \right)}+\frac{\text{Cx}+\text{D}}{\left( 2{{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)}$

note here that both numerators are linear because denominator in both factors is quadratic.

${{\text{x}}^{2}}+1=\left( \text{Ax}+\text{B} \right)\left( 2{{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)+\left( \text{Cx}+\text{D} \right)\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+2 \right)$

${{\text{x}}^{2}}+1=\left( 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}+\text{C} \right){{\text{x}}^{3}}+\left( 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}+\text{D} \right){{\text{x}}^{2}}+\left( \text{A}+2\text{C} \right)\text{x}+\text{B}+2\text{D}$

equating terms on both sides

$2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}+\text{C}=0$ … (i)

$2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}+\text{D}=1$ … (ii)

$\text{A}+2\text{C}=0$ … (iii)

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}+2\text{D}=1$ … (iv)

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}=0,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}=1/3,\text{C}=0,\text{D}=1/3$

$\therefore $I $~=\frac{1/3}{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+2 \right)}\text{dx}+\int ~\frac{1/3\text{dx}}{\left( 2{{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)}$

$~=\frac{1}{3}\int ~\frac{\text{dx}}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+2}+\frac{1}{3}\int ~\frac{\text{dx}}{2\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+1/2 \right)}$

$~=\frac{1}{3}{{\left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right)}^{\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}}}\frac{\text{x}}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{6}\left( \sqrt{2} \right)\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}\sqrt{2}+\text{C}$

Note: In quadratic factor part we have not used the trick method as it is applicable only when all factors are linear.

concepts.title: Using the method of partial fraction for solving integrals when the denominator has repeated linear or quadratic roots
concepts.conceptBody:
Using the method of partial fraction for solving integrals when the denominator has repeated linear or quadratic roots

When repeating factors are present i.e. when denominator is of the form

$\text{D}\left( \text{x} \right)={{(\text{x}-\alpha )}^{\text{k}1}}{{(\text{x}-\beta )}^{\text{k}2}}\ldots $ {for linear factor$\}$

$={{\left( a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c \right)}^{k1}}{{\left( p{{x}^{2}}+qx+c \right)}^{k}}$ for quadratic

(1) If function is linear.

i.e. $\frac{N\left( x \right)}{\left( x-a \right){{(x-b)}^{2}}{{(x-c)}^{3}}}$ we write it as following

$=\frac{\text{A}}{\left( \text{x}-\text{a} \right)}+\frac{{{\text{B}}_{1}}}{\left( \text{x}-\text{b} \right)}+\frac{{{\text{B}}_{2}}}{{{(\text{x}-\text{b})}^{2}}}+\frac{{{\text{C}}_{1}}}{\left( \text{x}-\text{c} \right)}+\frac{{{\text{C}}_{2}}}{{{(\text{x}-\text{c})}^{2}}}+\frac{{{\text{C}}_{3}}}{{{(\text{x}-\text{c})}^{3}}}$

i.e. if $\left( x-b \right)$ has power 2 then there will be two terms for it, $\left( x-b \right)\And {{(x-b)}^{2}}$ $\And $ if $\left( \text{x}-\text{c} \right)$ has power 3 , then 3 terms i.e. $\left( \text{x}-\text{c} \right),{{(\text{x}-\text{c})}^{2}}\And {{(\text{x}-\text{c})}^{3}}$

First Method 

It is the normal method of equating coefficients of equal degree on both sides. Though it might become complicated as the degree of factors increase.

Second (Trick) Method 

By this method we can find the values of $A,{{B}_{2}}\And {{C}_{3}}$ as we did in the Trick method in case of linear factors.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{A}={{\left. \frac{\text{N}\left( \text{x} \right)}{{{(\text{x}-\text{b})}^{2}}{{(\text{x}-\text{c})}^{3}}} \right|}_{\text{x}=\text{a}}}=\frac{\text{N}\left( \text{a} \right)}{{{(\text{a}-\text{b})}^{2}}{{(\text{a}-\text{c})}^{3}}} \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{B}}_{2}}={{\left. \frac{\text{N}\left( \text{x} \right)}{{{(\text{x}-\text{a})}^{2}}{{(\text{x}-\text{c})}^{3}}} \right|}_{\text{x}=\text{b}}}=\frac{\text{N}\left( \text{b} \right)}{{{(\text{b}-\text{a})}^{2}}{{(\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}-\text{c})}^{3}}} \\ {} & {{\text{C}}_{3}}={{\left. \frac{\text{N}\left( \text{x} \right)}{{{(\text{x}-\text{a})}^{2}}{{(\text{x}-\text{b})}^{3}}} \right|}_{\text{x}=\text{c}}}=\frac{\text{N}\left( \text{c} \right)}{\left( \text{c}-\text{a} \right){{(\text{c}-\text{b})}^{2}}} \\ \end{array}$

The values of ${{\text{B}}_{1}},{{\text{C}}_{1}}\And {{\text{C}}_{2}}$ are found as in the previous method ie., by comparing the coefficients on both sides.

(2) If function has quadratic factors 

i.e. of the form $\frac{N\left( x \right)}{\left( a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c \right){{\left( p{{x}^{2}}+qx+c \right)}^{2}}}$ etc.

we represent this function as following

$=\frac{Ax+B}{a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c}+\frac{{{P}_{1}}x+{{Q}_{1}}}{\left( p{{x}^{2}}+qx+r \right)}+\frac{{{P}_{2}}x+{{Q}_{2}}}{\left( p{{x}^{2}}+qx+{{r}^{2}} \right)}$

There is no trick method for this type, use the standard method of equating coefficients.

Q) (a) $\int \frac{1}{{{x}^{3}}{{(x-1)}^{2}}\left( x+1 \right)}dx$ 
(b) $\int \frac{\left( {{x}^{2}}+1 \right)xdx}{{{\left( {{x}^{2}}-1 \right)}^{2}}\left( x-3 \right)}$ 

Solution: 

(a) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\int \frac{\text{dx}}{{{\text{x}}^{3}}{{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}\left( \text{x}+1 \right)}$

as stated in formula part

$\frac{1}{{{\text{x}}^{3}}{{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}\left( \text{x}+1 \right)}=\frac{{{\text{A}}_{1}}}{\text{x}}+\frac{{{\text{A}}_{2}}}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}+\frac{{{\text{A}}_{3}}}{{{\text{x}}^{3}}}+\frac{{{\text{B}}_{1}}}{\left( \text{x}-1 \right)}+\frac{{{\text{B}}_{2}}}{{{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}}+\frac{\text{C}}{\left( \text{x}+1 \right)}$

by trick method we can find the values of ${{\text{A}}_{3}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{B}}_{2}}\And \text{C}$

${{\text{A}}_{3}}={{\left. \frac{1}{{{(\text{x}-1)}^{3}}\left( \text{x}+1 \right)} \right|}_{\text{x}=0}}=+1$

${{\text{B}}_{2}}={{\left. \frac{1}{{{\text{x}}^{3}}\left( \text{x}+1 \right)} \right|}_{\text{x}=1}}=\frac{1}{2}$

$\text{C}={{\left. \frac{1}{{{\text{x}}^{3}}{{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}} \right|}_{\text{x}=-1}}=\frac{-1}{4}$

for ${{A}_{1}},{{A}_{2}},{{B}_{1}}$ we have to use normal method

$1\equiv {{\text{A}}_{1}}\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}} \right){{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}\left( \text{x}+1 \right)+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{x}{{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}\left( \text{x}+1 \right)+$

${{\text{A}}_{3}}{{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}\left( \text{x}+1 \right)+{{\text{B}}_{1}}\left( {{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)\left( \text{x}-1 \right)\left( \text{x}+1 \right)$

$+{{\text{B}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)\left( \text{x}+1 \right)+\text{c}\left( {{\text{x}}^{3}} \right){{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}$

equating powers on both side 
${{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{B}}_{1}}+\text{C}=0$ ... (i) by equating ${{x}^{5}}$ 
$-{{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}+{{\text{B}}_{2}}+2\text{C}=0$ ... (ii) by equating ${{x}^{4}}$ 
$-{{\text{A}}_{1}}-{{\text{A}}_{2}}+{{\text{B}}_{1}}-{{\text{B}}_{2}}+{{\text{B}}_{3}}+\text{C}=0$ ... (iii) by equating ${{x}^{3}}$

Solving them by putting the values of ${{\text{A}}_{3}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{B}}_{2}}\And \text{C}$

We get ${{\text{A}}_{1}}=2,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}_{2}}=1,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{B}}_{1}}=\frac{-7}{4}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\int \frac{2}{x}+\int \frac{dx}{{{x}^{2}}}+\int \frac{dx}{{{x}^{3}}}+\int \frac{-7/4}{\left( x-1 \right)}+\int \frac{1/2dx}{{{(x-1)}^{2}}}+\int \frac{-1/4}{x+1}$

$=2\text{log}\left| x \right|-\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{2{{x}^{2}}}-\frac{7}{4}\text{log}\left| x-1 \right|-\frac{1}{2}\frac{1}{\left( x-1 \right)}-\frac{1}{4}\text{log}\left| x+1 \right|$

(b) $I=\int \frac{\left( {{x}^{2}}+1 \right)xdx}{{{\left( {{x}^{2}}-1 \right)}^{2}}\left( {{x}^{2}}-3 \right)}$

first of all we can see here that if we substitute ${{x}^{2}}=t$ here the $xdx$ gets removed

$2\text{xdx}=\text{dt}$.

$I=\int \frac{\left( t+1 \right)dt}{2{{(t-1)}^{2}}\left( t-3 \right)}$

$=\frac{1}{2}\int \frac{\left( t+1 \right)dt}{{{(t-1)}^{2}}\left( t-3 \right)}$

now it becomes the normal question of Type 2 linear factors.

$\frac{\text{t}+1}{{{(\text{t}-1)}^{2}}\left( \text{t}-3 \right)}=\frac{{{\text{A}}_{1}}}{\left( \text{t}-1 \right)}+\frac{{{\text{A}}_{2}}}{{{(\text{t}-1)}^{2}}}+\frac{\text{C}}{\left( \text{t}-3 \right)}$

finding ${{\text{A}}_{2}}\And \text{C}$ by trick method

${{\text{A}}_{2}}={{\left. \frac{\text{t}+1}{\left( \text{t}-3 \right)} \right|}_{\text{t}=1}}=\frac{1+1}{1-3}=-1$

$\text{C}={{\left. \frac{\text{t}+1}{\left( \text{t}-1 \right)} \right|}_{\text{t}=3}}=\frac{4}{4}=1$

for ${{A}_{1}}$ equating powers

$\text{t}+1={{\text{A}}_{1}}\left( \text{t}-1 \right)\left( \text{t}-3 \right)+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\left( \text{t}-3 \right)+\text{C}{{(\text{t}-1)}^{2}}$

equating coefficients of ${{t}^{2}}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{A}}_{1}}+\text{C}=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & {{\text{A}}_{1}}=-1 \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ integral becomes

I $~=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \int ~\frac{-dt}{t-1}+\int ~\frac{-1dt}{{{(t-1)}^{2}}}+\int ~\frac{1}{\left( t-3 \right)}dt \right]$

$~=\frac{1}{2}\left[ -\text{log}\left| t-1 \right|+\frac{1}{t-1}+\text{log}\left| t-3 \right| \right]+C$

putting back the value of $t$

I$~=\frac{1}{2}\left[ -\text{log}\left| {{\text{x}}^{2}}-1 \right|+\text{log}\left| {{\text{x}}^{2}}-3 \right|+\frac{1}{\text{t}-1} \right]+\text{C}$
